meta.issuer_cn: 中科寒武纪科技股份有限公司

company.name_cn: 中科寒武纪科技股份有限公司
company.headquarters: Room 1601, 16/F, Tower D, Zhizhen Building, No. 7 Zhichun Road, Haidian District, Beijing

products.name: Terminal Intelligent Processor IP - Cambricon 1A
products.specs: Intelligent processor IP for terminal/edge smart devices; licensed to customers such as HiSilicon for integration into SoC chips

products.name: Terminal Intelligent Processor IP - Cambricon 1H
products.specs: Intelligent processor IP for terminal/edge smart devices; licensed to customers such as HiSilicon for integration into SoC chips

products.name: Cloud Intelligent Chip and Accelerator Card - Siyuan 100 (MLU100)
products.specs: Cloud-side AI inference chip and accelerator card; used in cloud servers and data centers

products.name: Cloud Intelligent Chip and Accelerator Card - Siyuan 290 (MLU290)
products.specs: Next-generation cloud-side AI chip; competes with NVIDIA A100/V100; expected to achieve scaled revenue in 2021

products.name: Edge Intelligent Chip and Accelerator Card - Siyuan 220 (MLU220)
products.specs: Edge-side AI chip and M.2 accelerator card; officially released November 2019; targeted at AI industry clients and internet companies; expected scaled shipments in 2020

products.name: Intelligent Computing Cluster System
products.specs: Integrated AI computing cluster system based on proprietary cloud-side AI chips and software; deployed in data centers and research institutions (e.g., Hengqin Advanced Intelligent Computing Platform)

products.name: Cambricon Neuware
products.specs: Proprietary foundational software platform supporting cloud-edge-terminal integrated AI workloads; covers training and inference; ecosystem still developing relative to NVIDIA CUDA

by_product.product: Cloud Intelligent Chip and Accelerator Card

key_risks: Short operating history (founded March 2016) with rapidly changing business structure and commercial model; uncertainty about sustainable operations and future development prospects
Continuous large R&D expenditures resulting in persistent losses; risk that cumulative unrecovered losses will further expand if business expansion and revenue growth are adversely affected

Heavy dependence on Huawei HiSilicon for IP licensing revenue (100%/97.94%/92.56% of terminal AI processor IP revenue in 2017/2018/2019); Huawei HiSilicon chose to develop its own AI chips since 2018, causing IP licensing revenue to decline 41.23% in 2019, with further decline expected in 2020
Limited market demand for intelligent processor IP; competition intensifying from NVIDIA, Intel, Qualcomm, MediaTek, Huawei HiSilicon, and ARM entering the terminal AI processor IP market

High concentration of cloud AI chip and accelerator card sales to related party Sugon (Zhongke Shuguang) at 80.94% of cloud chip revenue in 2019; risk of sharp revenue decline if Sugon reduces purchases and new non-related customers cannot be expanded in time
Slow customer expansion for cloud AI chips and accelerator cards due to lengthy certification, testing, adaptation and porting processes required by customers before adoption

Siyuan 290 cloud AI chip faces intense competition from NVIDIA A100/V100 and Huawei HiSilicon Ascend 910; NVIDIA has superior software ecosystem (CUDA), more mature sales network, and earlier customer adoption timeline
Uncertainty in edge AI chip (Siyuan 220) business: product launched November 2019, still in sample testing phase with customers; risk that scaled shipments in 2020 may not be achieved as planned

Sustainability risk of intelligent computing cluster system business: heavily concentrated in two projects (97.29% of 2019 revenue); limited backlog beyond one pending order worth RMB 185.71 million; risk of slowdown in downstream AI data center construction demand

Risk that existing orders and contracts cannot be executed on schedule due to COVID-19, industry policy changes, international political/economic environment, macroeconomic conditions, and downstream customer production/sales disruptions
Risk of adverse impact from cooperation with Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (ICT-CAS) on future business operations and independent market development capabilities
Technology risk: AI chip technology is still in early stages; risk of failure to keep pace with rapid technological change or develop competitive next-generation products
Financial risk: persistent losses and potential need for additional financing; risk of dilution or inability to raise funds on favorable terms
Supply chain and foundry risk: dependence on third-party chip foundries (fabless model); risk of supply disruptions or inability to obtain advanced process nodes
Risk related to IPO fundraising projects not achieving expected returns or not being completed on schedule

projects.name: Note: Specific use-of-proceeds details (project names, amounts) were referenced in the prospectus table of contents under 'Section II, Item VIII: Use of Fundraising Proceeds' but the detailed project breakdown was not included in the provided text excerpts. Exact figures are not available from the supplied document pages.
projects.focus: Based on context, proceeds are expected to be used for: R&D of next-generation AI chips (cloud, edge, terminal), software ecosystem development (Cambricon Neuware platform), market expansion and sales network buildout, and general working capital

executive_summary:
Cambricon Technologies is a Beijing-based artificial intelligence chip designer focused on intelligent processors for cloud, edge, and terminal applications, including its Siyuan series AI accelerator cards and licensed processor IP.

Financially, the company is pre-profit and burning cash. Revenue grew sharply from $1.1M in 2017 to $16.1M in 2018 (up 1,392%) and $61.3M in 2019 (up 279%), driven largely by AI processor IP licensing to Huawei HiSilicon and a large AI computing cluster project. Net losses attributable to shareholders reached $162.8M in 2019, heavily inflated by $130.2M in share-based compensation. The underlying operating loss excluding non-recurring items was $52.0M. Gross margins are not disclosed.

Cambricon is raising up to 10% of post-IPO shares (approximately 40.1 million shares) on the Shanghai Stock Exchange STAR Market, sponsored by CITIC Securities. Proceeds will fund next-generation AI chip R&D, software ecosystem development, and working capital. Notable pre-IPO investors include CITIC and CICC affiliated funds.

The three biggest risks are: first, near-total historical dependence on Huawei HiSilicon for IP licensing revenue, which is already declining as Huawei builds its own chips; second, extreme customer concentration with 81% of cloud chip revenue coming from related party Sugon; and third, intense competition from NVIDIA, whose mature CUDA software ecosystem gives it a substantial and durable advantage over Cambricon's still-developing Neuware platform.
